items.title: [🚀 Feature]: Use yaci-node-app snapshots in backend tests and CI to eliminate hard resets
items.description:
## Summary

Replace the current hard-reset and preparation-tx replay approach in backend
test suites with snapshot-based state loading via yaci-node-app. Each test suite
restores from a known-good snapshot instead of rebuilding state from scratch.

## Motivation

The existing CI setup requires a full hard reset of the local devnet between
test suites, followed by replaying all preparation transactions before each
suite can run. This makes test runs slow and brittle: any flakiness in the
preparation phase blocks downstream tests.

Yaci node app supports saving and restoring devnet state as snapshots. A snapshot
taken after all preparation transactions have been confirmed captures exactly
the state each test suite expects. Restoring from that snapshot is near-instant
and removes the coupling between test suites entirely.

## Proposed Solution

- Define a canonical "prepared state" snapshot that includes:
  - All funded test wallets
  - Any pre-issued certificates or datums required by test fixtures
  - UVerify backend in its expected initial state
- Update the test harness to restore from this snapshot at the start of each
  test suite rather than triggering a hard reset and replaying transactions
- Integrate snapshot restoration into the CI pipeline so each job starts from
  a clean, deterministic state
- Store the snapshot artifact in the repo or in CI artifact storage and
  regenerate it only when the preparation transaction set changes

items.title: [🚀 Feature]: TxFlow-based submission pipeline with step supplier to handle concurrent certificate submissions
items.description:
## Summary

Implement a TxFlow-based submission pipeline with a custom step supplier to
queue and chain certificate transactions correctly, preventing failures caused
by rapid sequential submissions that result in conflicting UTXO references.

## Problem

Each certificate issuance in UVerify requires updating an on-chain UTXO datum
(state update). If a user submits two certificates in quick succession, both
transactions may be built against the same input UTXO. The first transaction
consumes that UTXO and the second fails on-chain because its input no longer
exists.

This is not a batch submission scenario. It can happen with just two back-to-back
submissions from a single user. The root cause is that the second transaction is
built before the first is confirmed, so the UTXO it references is already spent.

## Proposed Solution

Introduce a submission pipeline backed by TxFlow that processes certificate
requests in order. Instead of reading the current UTXO datum state from the
chain for each transaction, a custom step supplier reads the output datum state
of the preceding step in the pipeline. This allows the next transaction to be
built correctly against the output of the previous one before either is confirmed
on-chain.

Reference: https://cardano-client.dev/docs/preview/txflow

The pipeline must also handle rollbacks. If a transaction in the chain is rolled
back, the supplier detects the rollback point and rebuilds all subsequent
transactions in the queue from that point forward using the restored datum state.

Key responsibilities of the step supplier:
- Maintain an in-memory queue of pending certificate payloads
- Track the expected output datum state after each step so the next step builds
  against it rather than the on-chain state
- Detect rollbacks and trigger a rebuild of the tail of the chain from the
  affected step onward
- Surface submission errors per certificate so callers get accurate feedback

items.title: [🚀 Feature]: CIP170 and identity support
items.description:
Add support for [CIP-0170](https://github.com/Kammerlo/CIPs/tree/feat/keri-cip/CIP-0170) — KERI-backed metadata attestations — to UVerify, so that file/data verifications can be cryptographically tied to a self-certifying KERI identifier (AID) in addition to the existing Cardano-only anchoring. Integrate with Veridian and a Signify agent to issue interaction events that anchor UVerify's content hashes.

### Motivation

UVerify currently proves *that* a file existed at a point in time by anchoring its hash on Cardano. CIP-170 lets us also prove *who* attests to it, using a portable, key-rotatable identifier that is independent of any single wallet or chain. This matters for several UVerify directions that are already in scope:

- **Digital Product Passports (DPP):** ESPR/Battery Regulation / CRMA flows expect a verifiable issuer identity behind every claim. A KERI AID gives us a long-lived issuer identity that survives wallet/key rotation, which a Cardano payment key alone does not.
- **Small craft / Mittelstand use cases:** Businesses can keep a stable organizational identity even as operational keys change.
- **Stronger verification UX:** A UVerify proof page can show "verified by AID `E...`" with key state from KERI, on top of the existing tx-level proof.

### Background

CIP-170 defines a metadata convention where a transaction carries both:
1. The original payload (e.g. UVerify's existing `674`/file-hash metadata, or a `721` NFT label), and
2. A `170` label containing a KERI interaction event (`ixn`) that anchors a Blake2b-256 digest of the payload's value.

A reference implementation exists at [keri-cardano-tx-attestation-frontend](https://github.com/Kammerlo/keri-cardano-tx-attestation-frontend) (Next.js + Mesh + Signify-TS) and is a useful blueprint.

Resulting metadata shape:

```json
{
  "170": {
    "t": "ATTEST",
    "i": "E...",          // KERI AID
    "d": "E...",          // KERI event digest
    "s": "0",             // sequence number (hex)
    "v": { "v": "1.0" }
  },
  "<original-label>": { /* original payload, preserved verbatim */ }
}
```
### Workflow 

1. User connects CIP-30 wallet (existing UVerify behavior).
2. User connects/configures a KERI identifier — either via a Veridian wallet or a Signify agent endpoint + AID alias. New "Identity settings" panel.
3. User uploads file / pastes hash as today.
4. UVerify computes the Blake2b-256 digest of the metadata value that will be embedded.
5. UVerify asks Signify/Veridian to issue an `ixn` interaction event anchoring that digest.
6. UVerify builds a tx with `{ <uverify-label>: <payload>, "170": <ixn-proof> }`.
7. User signs and submits with their Cardano wallet.
8. Verification page renders both the UVerify proof and the KERI proof (AID, event digest, sequence number, key state lookup).

### Verification logic

Extend the verifier so that for transactions containing label `170`, it additionally:
- Re-hashes the original payload value (Blake2b-256) and compares against `170.d`'s anchored digest.
- Resolves the AID via KERI (OOBI / witness / KEL lookup) and validates the event signature and sequence number.
- Surfaces clear UI states: "Anchored" (Cardano only), "Anchored + Identity verified" (both), "Identity mismatch" (failure).

### Technical considerations

- **Veridian vs Signify-TS:** Veridian is the user-facing wallet path; Signify-TS is the agent path used by the reference frontend. Decide whether UVerify supports one or both. Veridian is preferable for non-technical UVerify users (DPP, craft businesses); Signify is useful for server-side / institutional issuers.
- **KERI infrastructure:** We need at least one reachable witness/agent. Decide whether UVerify ships a default (operated by us / CF) or requires users to configure their own.
- **Hashing parity with the reference impl:** Blake2b-256 over the *value* of the metadata label (not the full CBOR map). Match the reference frontend's normalization exactly to keep cross-tool verification working.
- **CIP-170 status:** Still a draft PR. We should track upstream changes and pin our implementation to a specific revision until merge.
- **Bundle size / SSR:** Signify-TS and libsodium have non-trivial WASM/build implications in Next.js — worth a small spike before committing to the architecture.
- **Backwards compatibility:** Existing UVerify transactions must continue to verify unchanged. The `170` label is purely additive.

### Out of scope (for this issue)

- Issuing ACDC credentials (separate follow-up — relevant for full DPP).
- Multi-sig KERI identifiers.
- On-chain KEL anchoring beyond what CIP-170 specifies.

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] User can configure a KERI identifier (Veridian and/or Signify agent) in UVerify settings.
- [ ] new file verifications optionally produce a CIP-170-compliant transaction.
- [ ] Verifier validates both the Cardano-side proof and the KERI proof, and clearly surfaces the identity in the UI.
- [ ] Verification still works for legacy (non-CIP-170) UVerify transactions with no regression.
- [ ] Documentation updated: README, verification-page explainer, and a short user guide for setting up a KERI identifier.

### References

- CIP-0170 draft: https://github.com/Kammerlo/CIPs/tree/feat/keri-cip/CIP-0170
- KERI / Signify-TS: https://github.com/WebOfTrust/signify-ts
- Veridian: https://veridian.id

items.title: [🐞 Bug]: Backend did not respond for preprod without any error
items.description: We need to check if there is somewhere a blocking process. The logs were fine and blocks processed normally. The only issue was that curl and any other requests timed out. The issue was resolved after a restart of the service.

items.title: [🚀 Feature]: Claude Skill for UVerify SDKs and Custom UI Templates
items.description:
## Summary

Create a Claude skill document that enables AI coding assistants to accurately
help developers integrate the UVerify SDK and build custom certificate UI
templates, without hallucinating API signatures or misrepresenting platform
behavior.

## Motivation

As UVerify grows adoption among developers, more of them will reach for Claude
or similar assistants while integrating the SDK. Without a skill file, Claude
defaults to guessing based on similar libraries, producing incorrect code that
references non-existent methods or wrong datum structures.

A well-written skill closes this gap: it gives Claude the exact API surface,
correct on-chain datum schema, SDK initialization patterns for Node.js, Python,
and Java, and guidance on building custom UI templates for verification
certificates.

## Proposed Solution

Create a SKILL.md file at the root of the uverify-examples repo (or as a
standalone repository) covering:

**SDK usage**
- Installation and initialization for each supported runtime
  (Node.js, Python, Java)
- Issuing a certificate: required parameters, wallet integration (mainnet) vs
  headless wallet (server-side) vs SDK faucet (preprod)
- Verifying a certificate: wallet-free flow, expected response shape
- On-chain datum schema: field names, types, and semantics
- Common error patterns and how to handle them

**Custom UI templates**
- Structure of a certificate template: required fields, optional metadata
- How to pass custom template data at issuance time
- How to retrieve and render a certificate by hash
- Example templates with annotated code

**Platform-specific notes**
- Wallet requirements: CIP-30 on mainnet (Eternl, Lace, Nami), none for
  verification, headless for server-side issuance
- Preprod vs mainnet differences
- Blockfrost configuration

items.title: Local devnet playground for uverify-examples using yaci-node-app and snapshots
items.description:
## Summary

Integrate yaci-node-app into the uverify-examples repository so developers can
spin up a fully pre-funded local Cardano devnet from a snapshot and run all
SDK examples without a wallet, testnet ADA, or any external dependency.

## Motivation

The current developer onboarding path for uverify-examples requires either a
real preprod wallet or manual interaction with the SDK faucet at
app.preprod.uverify.io. This creates friction for developers who just want to
explore the SDK locally.

Yaci DevKit can launch a local devnet in seconds and supports loading from a
snapshot that already contains the required funding state and UVerify
preparation transactions. This would make the examples completely self-contained
and reproducible.

## Proposed Solution

- Add a yaci-node-app configuration to the uverify-examples repo
  (Docker Compose or npm-based, depending on the SDK language)
- Create a devnet snapshot that includes:
  - Pre-funded developer wallets used in the examples
  - Any preparation transactions required by the UVerify backend
- Add a README section with a one-command startup: spin up devnet, load
  snapshot, run examples
- Point the SDK examples to the local Blockfrost-compatible Yaci Store API
  endpoint by default when a local devnet is detected

items.title: [👷 Task]: UI Logic to Hide/Prevent Double Submission of Identical Hashes
items.description:
Currently, the UI allows users to submit the same hash multiple times, even if the certificate policy for the first submission should prevent or hide subsequent attempts.

### Requirements

- Implement a check against the certificate policy during the submission flow.
- If a hash has already been submitted and the policy dictates uniqueness, the UI should either disable the submit button or provide a warning/link to the existing certificate.
- For certificates not submitted via the ui, the certificate page should show only the first submission, pagination, the lastest submission or just paginations for the identical issuer based on the policy

items.title: [👷 Task]: Publish Artifact to Maven Central
items.description: Currently only the TypeScript artifacts have been pushed to npmjs, but the Java library still needs to be uploaded, so that it is accessible for maven and gradle users.

items.description: Currently only the TypeScript artifacts have been pushed to npmjs, but the Python library still needs to be uploaded, so that it is accessible for pip users.

items.title: [👷 Task]: Remove restricted ui templates from repository
items.description: Currently, the Tadamon/UNDP and Connected Goods templates are restricted to certain addresses. It makes sense to create an external repository for uverify-additional-templates that includes restricted templates, while the main UI package should contain only publicly available UI templates. After removing the Connected Goods template, it would make sense to create an adjustable, freely accessible version of it, along with other types of certificates.

items.title: API documentation
Modernize Landing Page and Integrate Live Data
items.description:
### Description

The current UVerify landing page is outdated and does not reflect the real-time activity of the network. We need to refresh the design and connect it to our statistics endpoint.

### Tasks

- Update the UI/UX design to match the current project branding.
- Implement data fetching from the `/statistics` endpoint for transaction and fee insights.
- Display live metrics such as total certificates issued
- Explain custom UI's and link docs

Improve Documentation for Cost Calculation and UI Submissions
items.description:
### Description

Our current documentation lacks depth in critical operational areas. New users struggle to predict costs and understand the custom UI submission flow.

### Content Needed

#### Cost Calculation Guide
- Detailed breakdown of fees, gas costs, and how they scale.
- Examples for different certificate volumes.
- Comparison of different deployment strategies.

#### Custom UI Submission Process
- A step-by-step tutorial on how to submit and integrate custom UIs within the UVerify ecosystem.
- Best practices for UI design.
- Security considerations.

items.title: Templating engine
Add privacy policy
Add terms of use
[🚀 Feature]: Add Whitelist File to Download External Ui Templates
items.description: Allow users to contribute custom UI configurations for standard deployments by submitting them with a repository hash. UVerify will try to download the repository matching the hash and whitelist this ui template for app.uverify.io. This enhances community contributions and UI flexibility while maintaining security through hash verification.

items.title: [🚀 Feature]: Library Endpoints to Improve Proxy Contract Upgrades
items.description: Currently the library updates need to be done manually and also the transaction has and output index for each submission needs to be part of the .env file, which makes the proxy update less intuitive and leaves space for issues during the process. Adding a library controller would improve this situation and a library service could pick up the latest state contract version on-the-fly.

items.title: [🐞 Bug]: Tadamon template has no restriction to grow on its y axis
items.description: Currently on big screens, the Tadamon template just grows in y direction without any restriction which could look odd in some situations
